blocks.text: So when Peter went up to Jerusalem, the circumcised believers criticized him,
2 : Up to this point all believers were (see note ; at 10.45 the text says “circumcised believers,” but the meaning is the same, as v. 18 makes clear).
saying, “Why did you go to uncircumcised men and eat with them?”
3 : The question is framed in terms of association with Gentiles, not their entry into the church; cf. Gal 2.12.
Then Peter began to explain it to them, step by step, saying,
“I was in the city of Joppa praying, and in a trance I saw a vision. There was something like a large sheet coming down from heaven, being lowered by its four corners; and it came close to me.
As I looked at it closely I saw four-footed animals, beasts of prey, reptiles, and birds of the air.
I also heard a voice saying to me, ‘Get up, Peter; kill and eat.’
But I replied, ‘By no means, Lord; for nothing profane or unclean has ever entered my mouth.’
But a second time the voice answered from heaven, ‘What God has made clean, you must not call profane.’
This happened three times; then everything was pulled up again to heaven.
At that very moment three men, sent to me from Caesarea, arrived at the house where we were.
The Spirit told me to go with them and not to make a distinction between them and us. These six brothers also accompanied me, and we entered the man's house.
12 : , clarifies “go … without hesitation” in 10.20. , replaces “some” in 10.23.
He told us how he had seen the angel standing in his house and saying, ‘Send to Joppa and bring Simon, who is called Peter;
he will give you a message by which you and your entire household will be saved.’
14 : Unlike 10.33, here it is made clear that Cornelius is expecting a of salvation (4.12).
And as I began to speak, the Holy Spirit fell upon them just as it had upon us at the beginning.
And I remembered the word of the Lord, how he had said, ‘John baptized with water, but you will be baptized with the Holy Spirit.’
If then God gave them the same gift that he gave us when we believed in the Lord Jesus Christ, who was I that I could hinder God?”
4–17 : answers by retelling 10.1–48 in abbreviated fashion.
When they heard this, they were silenced. And they praised God, saying, “Then God has given even to the Gentiles the repentance that leads to life.”
11.1–18: Peter's defense. The Jerusalem church requires an explanation for the baptism of Gentiles.
17–18 : The “Gentile Pentecost” (v. 15; 10.47; 15.8) was an act of Although the issue of Gentile inclusion seems to be decisively settled, controversy will resurface in ch 15.
Now those who were scattered because of the persecution that took place over Stephen traveled as far as Phoenicia, Cyprus, and Antioch, and they spoke the word to no one except Jews.

19 : on the Orontes River, capital of the Roman province of Syria, which included Galilee and Judea, and according to Josephus ( 3.29), the third largest city (after Rome and Alexandria) in the Roman Empire.
But among them were some men of Cyprus and Cyrene who, on coming to Antioch, spoke to the Hellenists also, proclaiming the Lord Jesus.
20 : , a great North African city (2.10; 6.9; 13.1; Lk 23.26), had a large Jewish colony. (unlike 6.1 and 9.29) here apparently refers to non-Jewish, Greek-speaking residents of
The hand of the Lord was with them, and a great number became believers and turned to the Lord.
News of this came to the ears of the church in Jerusalem, and they sent Barnabas to Antioch.
When he came and saw the grace of God, he rejoiced, and he exhorted them all to remain faithful to the Lord with steadfast devotion;
for he was a good man, full of the Holy Spirit and of faith. And a great many people were brought to the Lord.

22–24 : came from Cyprus (4.36), and there were Cypriots in Antioch (v. 20). He investigates and expresses approval over the latest developments on behalf of (cf. 8.14–17; 11.1–18). Compare his description (v. 24) with that of Stephen (6.5).
Then Barnabas went to Tarsus to look for Saul,

and when he had found him, he brought him to Antioch. So it was that for an entire year they met with the church and taught a great many people, and it was in Antioch that the disciples were first called “Christians.”
11.19–26: Mission to the Greeks in Antioch. The narrative now rejoins the mission initiated by the following Stephen's death (8.1,4).

25–26 : was in (9.30), cf. Gal 1.21. An extended collaboration with Barnabas is initiated (see Gal 2.1,9,13; 1 Cor 9.6). , a Latin word meaning “partisans of Christ,” perhaps at first a term of reproach. It occurs elsewhere in the New Testament only at 26.28 and 1 Pet 4.16. It appears to be a term gaining currency in Luke's day that has been retrojected into the time portrayed by the narrative.
At that time prophets came down from Jerusalem to Antioch.
27 : (13.1; 15.32) were numerous in the early church and in Acts characterize the last days (2.17–18). Here they predict the future (see 21.10).
One of them named Agabus stood up and predicted by the Spirit that there would be a severe famine over all the world; and this took place during the reign of Claudius.
28 : , 21.10–11. The probably occurred in 47 ; it was not worldwide (see Josephus, 20.51–53,101). The Roman emperor ruled 41–54 .
The disciples determined that according to their ability, each would send relief to the believers living in Judea;
29 : The operation resembles the arrangements for Paul's collection (20.4n.; 1 Cor 16.2; 2 Cor 9.7); the days of selling all (2.44–45; 4.32–35) are gone.
this they did, sending it to the elders by Barnabas and Saul.
11.27–30: Antioch aids Jerusalem.

30 : , as Christian leaders are first mentioned here (“old men” in 2.17 translates the same Greek word); their emergence may reflect synagogue practice. According to Gal 2.1–10, Paul's second visit to Jerusalem coincided with the discussion narrated in Acts 15.1–29.
